elements.sent-2411-007: One of the main aims in the field of dynamical systems is to determine which dynamical systems are stable, and if possible, to prove that such systems are {\lq typical{\rq .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: We will address this question in a setting where systems are never structurally stable, as explained below.
elements.sent-2411-009: For this reason, we define a map to be {\em stable in this context if the next best thing holds: if -loosely speaking- its non-wandering set depends continuously on the system.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Note that this notion does not require that the system and its perturbation are conjugate on their non-wandering sets.
elements.sent-2411-011: This paper is concerned with the space of Interval Translation Maps ($\ITM$'s), defined below.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this setting, the non-wandering can be equal to a finite union of intervals, but it can also be equal to a Cantor set.
elements.sent-2411-013: This class generalizes the well-known class of Interval Exchange Transformations ($\IET$'s).
elements.sent-2411-014: For $\IET$'s, the non-wandering set is always the entire interval and the main result of this paper becomes trivial.
elements.sent-2411-015: That it holds for $\ITM$'s is proved in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-016: This result implies in particular that stable maps are simple: their dynamics on their non-wandering set corresponds to (a union of) circle rotations.
elements.sent-2411-017: Whether or not a map in $\ITM$ is stable is equivalent to two conditions: absence of critical connections (ACC) and the matching condition (M).
elements.sent-2411-018: Here ACC means that no iterate of a discontinuity (i.e. a critical point) is mapped to another critical point.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the $\IET$ literature this is often called the Keane condition.
elements.sent-2411-020: The condition M means that the return map to a component of the non-wandering set has at most one discontinuity.
elements.sent-2411-021: This condition has no clear analogue for invertible systems.
elements.sent-2411-022: It rules out that one connected component of the non-wandering set consists of two pieces with independent dynamics which join up for {\lq coincidental{\rq reasons.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The corresponding question for systems on the complex plane (such as quadratic maps) is the Fatou conjecture and is still wide open.
elements.sent-2411-024: Hence the ACC and M conditions can be considered as the analogues of hyperbolicity in our setting.
elements.sent-2411-025: The main tool in this paper is a transversality result, namely the linear independence of certain critical itinerary vectors.
elements.sent-2411-026: It is of course easy to determine how changing the intervals and translations changes the local dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-027: The challenge is to determine what happens with the global dynamics when we change some chosen local dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-028: To show that we have the required freedom to control the global dynamics, we need to establish the linear independence of certain vectors associated to the orbits of critical points (critical itinerary vectors).
elements.sent-2411-029: The proof of this linear independence relies on a rather detailed and subtle dynamical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-030: A map $T:[0,1)\to [0,1)$ is called an Interval Translation Mapping ($\ITM$) if there exists a partition of $I = [0,1)$ into finitely many intervals $I_i=[\beta_{i-1 ,\beta_{i )$, where $0=\beta_0<\beta_1<\dots < \beta_{r-1 <\beta_r=1$ and $1 \le i \le r$, such that the restriction of $T$ to each of these intervals is a translation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In other words, we have that: \begin{equation}T\vert_{I_i}(x) = x+\gamma_i,\end{equation} for $1 \le i \le r$.

elements.sent-2411-086: The basic problem we deal with everywhere in the paper is: how to perturb a map $T$ so that some of its dynamical properties change, while some others do not? In analogy to smooth one-dimensional dynamics (e.g. \cite{MR1239171 ), the dynamical properties of a map depend on the behaviour of critical points.}
elements.sent-2411-087: For us, the discontinuities of the map serve as the critical points.

elements.subsection-2411-004: History of $\ITM$'s, $\IET$'s and the dynamics of piecewise isometries
elements.sent-2411-091: The subject of interval exchange transformations (IETs) goes back to the 70's.
elements.sent-2411-092: Some the earliest publications were by Katok \cite{MR0331438 , Keane \cite{MR0357739 , \cite{MR0435353 and Veech \cite{MR0516048 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: As the literature on IETs is huge we will only refer to the survey papers \cite{MR2219821 , \cite{MR2648692 .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: It is well-known that IET correspond to billiards, see for example \cite{MR0399423 and \cite{MR0644840 , \cite[Ch.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: In 1995, Boshernitzan and Kornfeld \cite{MR1356616 introduced interval translation maps as a natural generalisation of $\IET$'s.}
elements.sent-2411-096: The main surprise in their paper was an explicit example of an $\ITM$ with three branches which is of infinite type.
elements.sent-2411-097: They also mentioned that $\ITM$'s correspond to billiards with {\lq one-sided mirrors{\rq .}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Such billiards were considered in more detail by \cite{MR3449199 ; see Appendix~\ref{appendix:billiards .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: In 1993, Levitt \cite{MR1231840 also considered a two-parameter family of $\ITM$'s, related to the holonomy map of certain 1-dimensional foliations in two dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Bruin and Troubetzkoy \cite{MR2013352 showed that one can use a renormalisation approach to study a special two-parameter family of $\ITM$'s quite systematically.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Using this approach they show that within their family the set of parameters corresponding to infinite type maps is uncountable and has Lebesgue measure zero.
elements.sent-2411-102: Volk \cite{MR3124735 generalised this result by showing that within the space of $\ITM$'s with three intervals the set of infinite type maps has Lebesgue measure zero.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Schmeling and Troubetzkoy \cite{MR1796167 obtained several results for general $\ITM$'s.}
elements.sent-2411-104: In \cite{MR2966738 and \cite{MR2152403 , considered a special class of $\ITM$'s on $4$ intervals called double rotations.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The notion of Matching also appears in previous works, see for example \cite{MR3893724 , \cite{MR3597033 , \cite{MR2422375 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: Interestingly, the authors were unaware of this at the time of defining Matching in the context of this paper.

elements.sent-2411-108: In \cite{MR4082258 the authors connected 1D and 2D piecewise isometries, by embedding $\IET$'s into planar piecewise isometries.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The structure of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-110: In Section $2$ we prove that $\overline{X}$ is equal to the non-wandering set of and a few other auxiliary results used throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-111: In Section $3$ we introduce the product notation, define eventually periodic maps and prove Theorem $A$.
elements.sent-2411-112: In Section $4$ we prove the crucial Theorem \ref{thm:lin-dep about the linear dependence of critical itinerary vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In Section $5$ we define the ACC and Matching conditions and prove Theorem $B$, using a special case of Theorem \ref{thm:lin-dep , while in Section $6$ we prove Theorem $C$, using the full version of Theorem \ref{thm:lin-dep .}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In Section $7$ we discuss the parameter space and possible future research.
elements.sent-2411-115: Finally, we discuss the connections with billiards in the appendix.

elements.section-2411-003: Product notation and eventually periodic maps
elements.sent-2411-268: In this subsection, we introduce the product notation, which will be used throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-269: It is a very convenient way to represent and control iterates of critical points.
elements.sent-2411-270: For each $T$, $x \in I$, $s=1,\dots,r$ and $n\ge 1$, we define: \begin{equation}k_s(x,n,T) := \# \{ T^i(x) \in I_s; 0\le i < n \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-271: Thus $k_s(x,n,T)$ represents the number of entries of $x$ into $I_s$ up to time $n$.
elements.sent-2411-272: This is an analogue of the first term in the continued fraction expansion for circle rotations, see \cite[Section 1.1]{MR1239171 .}
elements.sent-2411-273: When the map $T$ is clear from the context, we will usually simply write $k_s(x,n)$.
elements.sent-2411-274: Let $W(r) := \R{ ^{r \bigoplus \R{ ^{r-1}$ be the $(2r-1)$-dimensional real vector space that contains the coefficient vectors (introduced below), and let $(\bm{e _1, \dots, \bm{e _r)$ and $(\bm{f _1, \dots, \bm{f _{r-1 )$ be the canonical bases for $\R{ ^{r}$ and $\R{ ^{r-1}$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-275: Recall that $\ITM(r)$ is the parameter space of $\ITM$'s on $r$ intervals, and that it is a convex polytope contained in $\mathbb{R ^{2r-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-276: We call the elements of this space parameter vectors.
elements.sent-2411-277: These vectors also have canonical coordinates coming from the ambient space $\mathbb{R ^{2r-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-278: We will use the shorthand $(\gamma \, \beta)$ for a parameter vector $(\gamma_1 \dots \gamma_r \, \beta_1 \dots \beta_{r-1 )$.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Let $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle$ be the standard scalar product on $\mathbb{R ^{2r-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Since $W(r) = \R{ ^{r \bigoplus \R{ ^{r-1}$ and $\ITM(r)$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R ^{2r-1}$, it makes sense to write $\langle v, (\gamma \, \beta) \rangle = \sum_{s=1 ^r v_s \gamma_s + \sum_{s=1 ^{r-1 v_{s+r \beta_s$ for a coefficient vector $v = \sum_{s=1 ^r v_s \bm{e _s + \sum_{s=1 ^{r-1 v_{s+r \bm{f _s \in W(r)$ and a parameter vector $(\gamma \, \beta) \in \ITM(r)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-281: We call $\langle v, (\gamma \, \beta) \rangle$ the product of $v$ and $(\gamma \, \beta)$.
elements.sent-2411-282: The first reason for introducing this product is to obtain a formula for any iterate $T^n(\betas)$ of some discontinuity $\betas$.
elements.sent-2411-283: Define the vector of coefficients in $v(\betas,n,T) \in W(r)$ of this iterate as: \begin{equation}v(\betas,n,T) \coloneqq \left(\sum_{1 \le s \le r} k_s(\betas, n, T) \, e_s, \, f_{ind(\betas)}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}v(\betas,n,T) \coloneqq \left(\sum_{1 \le s \le r} k_s(\betas, n, T) \, e_s, \, f_{ind(\betas)}\right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-284: Then the following holds: \begin{equation}T^n(\betas) = \sum_{s=1}^r k_s(\betas,n,T) \gamma_s + \betas = \langle \, v(\betas,n,T) , (\gamma \, \beta) \, \rangle,\end{equation} where the vector $(\gamma \, \beta)$ corresponds to the defining coefficients of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-285: With this notation, we can associate to any landing of some discontinuity $\betas$ on some other discontinuity $\betass$ a coefficient vector in the following way.

elements.sent-2411-755: The definition of stability mainly concerns the topology and the location of the non-wandering set.
elements.sent-2411-756: Thus the dynamical meaning of stability is unclear from the definition, and the dynamical information retained by making a small perturbation of a stable map is relatively soft.
elements.sent-2411-757: We would therefore like to characterize stability in terms of more concrete dynamical properties.
elements.sent-2411-758: Intuitively speaking, three things can happen for an arbitrarily small perturbation $\Tilde{T}$ of a map $T$ that can break stability:.
elements.sent-2411-759: A definite part of $X$ gets removed, i.e. $\tilde{X}$ is smaller (either by size or by the number of intervals) than $X$ by a definite amount, so the mapping $\overline{X}$ is not lower semi-continuous.
elements.sent-2411-760: A definite new part gets added to $X$, i.e. $\Tilde{X}$ is bigger (either by size or by the number of intervals) than $X$ by a definite amount, so the mapping $\overline{X}$ is not upper semi-continuous.
elements.sent-2411-761: Discontinuities that were in $X$ are not in $\Tilde{X}$.
elements.sent-2411-762: We are therefore looking for dynamical properties that guarantee that none of these three things can happen for an arbitrarily small perturbation of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-763: To guarantee that the first thing does not happen, we can ask for the dynamics of the full orbit of every interval $J$ of $X$ to remain the same after perturbation (as in the example above).
elements.sent-2411-764: For the third, we can simply require that there are no discontinuities in the boundary of $X$ (assuming the first and the second things do not happen).
elements.sent-2411-765: The second one is the most elusive, and we will need to discuss it a bit before giving the definitions of the required dynamical properties.
elements.sent-2411-766: The following example illustrates the simplest way in which the set $X$ can become larger.
elements.sent-2411-767: Assume that there are two discontinuities $\betas$ and $\betass$ for which there exist $k_1 > 0$ and $k_2 > 0$ such that: \begin{equation}T^{k_1}(\betas^+) = \betass^+  and  T^{k_2}(\betass^-) = \betas^-.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-768: Moreover, assume that none of the discontinuities $\betas^+, \betas^-, \betass^+$ and $\betass^-$ are in $X$ and that there exists a perturbation $\Tilde{T}$ of $T$ such that:.
elements.sent-2411-769: The itinerary of $\betas^+$ remains the same up to time $k_1$ and at time $k_1$ it lands $\epsilon$-distance to the left of $\betass^-$;.
elements.sent-2411-770: The itinerary of $\betass^-$ remains the same up to time $k_2$ and at time $k_2$ it lands $\epsilon$-distance to the right of $\betas^+$;.
elements.sent-2411-771: Note that there exists an interval of points to the right of $\betas^+$ all of which have the same itinerary as $\betas^+$ up to time $k_1$.
elements.sent-2411-772: The same holds for $\betass^-$ and its itinerary up to time $k_2$.
elements.sent-2411-773: In particular, these intervals of definite size map forward continuously up to times $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-774: By our assumption on the perturbation $\Tilde{T}$, for a sufficiently small $\epsilon$ the interval $[\betas^+,\betas^+ + \epsilon)$ maps forward continuously up to time $k_1 + k_2$.
elements.sent-2411-775: Thus it is periodic with period $k_1 + k_2$ and is thus contained in $\Tilde{X}$.
elements.sent-2411-776: As $\betas^+, \betas^-, \betass^+$ and $\betass^-$ were by assumption not in $X$ and therefore a definite distance away from it, we see that $\overline{X (\Tilde{T )$ and $\overline{X (T)$ cannot be close.}}}
elements.sent-2411-777: Thus $X$ can not be a stable map.
elements.sent-2411-778: This example is the simplest case of a more general phenomenon called ghost preimages.
elements.sent-2411-779: We give the definition for a $+$-type discontinuity, with the one for $-$-type being analogous.
elements.sent-2411-780: A discontinuity $\betass^-$ that lands on $\betas^-$ is called a ghost preimage of $\betas^+$.
elements.sent-2411-781: Thus a ghost preimage of a $+$-type discontinuity $\betas^+$ is a $-$-type discontinuity $\betass^-$ that lands on $\betas^-$.
elements.sent-2411-782: The definition can easily be extended to a $-$-type discontinuity.
elements.sent-2411-783: The motivation for the name is the following: $\betass^-$ is almost a preimage of $\betas^+$, i.e. by an arbitrarily small perturbation we can make the iterate $T^k(\betass^-)$ land to the right of $\betas^+$, thus creating an actual preimage of $\betas^+$.
elements.sent-2411-784: In Example \ref{ex:ghost-preimage we have that $\betas^+$ is a ghost preimage of $\betass^-$ and that $\betass^-$ is a ghost preimage of $\betas^+$ as well.}
elements.sent-2411-785: This is the property that allows for the enlargement of $X$, and it motivates the following definition:.
elements.sent-2411-786: The ghost tree $\mathcal{GT (\beta)$ of $\beta$ is defined inductively in the following way.}
elements.sent-2411-787: Set $\beta$ to be the root of the tree, i.e. the set of level $0$ vertices of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-788: Assume that we have defined all of the vertices of level $\le n$ and all of the edges between them.
elements.sent-2411-789: Then the level $n+1$ vertices of the tree correspond to the set of all ghost preimages (if such exist) of the discontinuities corresponding to level $n$ vertices of the tree.
elements.sent-2411-790: The new edges are those between discontinuities and their ghost preimages, i.e. a new direct edge $\betas \to \betass$ is added if and only if $\betas$ is a level $n+1$ vertex, $\betass$ is a level $n$ vertex and $\betas$ is a ghost preimage of $\betass$.
elements.sent-2411-791: Thus $\mathcal{GT (\beta)$ is a directed tree, with well-defined levels, and edges exist only between vertices of consecutive levels.}
elements.sent-2411-792: If $\betas \to \betass$, then we say that $\betass$ is a parent of $\betas$ and that $\betas$ is a child of $\betass$.
elements.sent-2411-793: The types of discontinuities alternate between consecutive levels and the tree can have finitely or infinitely many levels.
elements.sent-2411-794: The following simple lemma gives a criterion for when a ghost tree is infinite:.
elements.sent-2411-795: Note that this is exactly the case in Example \ref{ex:ghost-preimage .}
elements.sent-2411-796: If $\beta$ appears again as a vertex on some level $n > 0$, then all of the levels that appeared before the second appearance of $\beta$ have to repeat.
elements.sent-2411-797: There is at least one level between these appearances, as the discontinuity type has to change from level to level.
elements.sent-2411-798: Thus the tree must be infinite.
elements.sent-2411-799: We are now ready to state the two dynamical properties that characterize stability.
elements.sent-2411-800: All of our statements work for all components $J$ of $X$, except in the special case when no point in the interior of $J$ lands on a discontinuity and the return map $R_J$ to this component is the identity.
elements.sent-2411-801: We will call such components of $X$ dynamically trivial, and components not of this form dynamically non-trivial.
elements.sent-2411-802: The reason for the name is that if any of these properties is violated, then the defining parameters $(\gamma \, \beta)$ of $T$ satisfy a non-trivial linear equation which we call a critical connection.
elements.sent-2411-803: Note that these are particular critical connections that we want to avoid and that we do not need to avoid all of them.
elements.sent-2411-804: An easy consequence of A1 and A2 is that there are no discontinuities in the boundary of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-805: Indeed, the preimage in $X$ such a discontinuity is either a discontinuity, which violates A1, or a boundary point of a dynamically non-trivial interval of $X$, which violates A2.
elements.sent-2411-806: Note that if A1 and A2 hold, then every vertex of the ghost tree $\mathcal{GT (\beta)$ for a $\beta \notin X$ corresponds to a discontinuity that is not contained in $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-807: Indeed, by the above, we have that there are no discontinuities in the boundary.
elements.sent-2411-808: Thus if $\beta_2^-$ is a ghost preimage of $\beta_1^+$, and $\beta_2^-$ is contained in $X$, this means that $\beta_1^+$ is also contained in $X$.
elements.sent-2411-809: Thus every ghost preimage of a $\beta \notin X$ must also not be in $X$.
elements.sent-2411-810: Thus the claim follows by induction.
elements.sent-2411-811: The reason we call this property Matching is that if $a$ is the single point from above, then we must have that:.
elements.sent-2411-812: An analogous property also called Matching has been discussed in slightly different contexts (see \cite{MR3893724 , \cite{MR3597033 , \cite{MR2422375 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-813: In the case when no point in the interior of $J$ lands on a discontinuity, we know that $J$ is a maximal periodic interval and thus both boundary points of $J$ land on discontinuities.
elements.sent-2411-814: If A1 and A2 hold for $T$, then the boundary points of such intervals only land on discontinuities and are not actually themselves discontinuities of $T$, since there are no discontinuities in the boundary of $X$, by the discussion below Definition \ref{defn:acc .}
elements.sent-2411-815: With the definitions out of the way, we can now recall the statement of Theorem B:.
elements.sent-2411-816: The proof of Theorem \ref{thm:stability=accm is spread over the next three subsections.}
elements.sent-2411-817: The outline of the proof is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-818: For the `only if' direction, we will show that a stable map $T$ that already satisfies A1 and A2 must also satisfy Matching.
elements.sent-2411-819: We will then show that any nearby map must also satisfy A1 and A2, which shows that any stable map must satisfy A1 and A2.
elements.sent-2411-820: If a stable map does not satisfy A3, we will be able to produce an explicit perturbation of the map that increases the non-wandering set by a definite size, which gives a contradiction.
elements.sent-2411-821: Putting all of this together shows that stability implies ACC and Matching.
elements.sent-2411-822: For the other direction, we will show that the ACC and Matching properties are very rigid, and that therefore any map close to such a map must also satisfy these properties, giving stability.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Stability, A1 and A2 imply Matching
elements.sent-2411-823: Recall that $k_s(x)$ is the number of entries of the orbit of $x$ to the interval $I_s$ up to, but not including, time $k$: \begin{equation}k_s(x) \coloneqq \# \{ T^j(x) \in I_s  for  0 \le j < n \}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-824: With this definition, we have the following explicit formula for any iterate of $x$: \begin{equation}T^k(x) = x + \sum_{s=1}^r k_s(x) \gamma_s.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-825: We now recall the notation for the dynamics of a return map $R_J$ to $J$, from the beginning of Section 4.
elements.sent-2411-826: The notation is slightly changed, so that it better fits the notation of this section.
elements.sent-2411-827: Let $J = [x,y)$ be a dynamically non-trivial interval of $X$ and let $a_1, a_2, \dots a_{N-1}$ be the points in $J$ which land on discontinuities before returning to $J$ and let $l_1, l_2, \dots, l_N$ be the corresponding landing times on critical points.

elements.sent-2411-1088: In this subsection, we formalise this idea and prove a few preliminary lemmas, before going into the proof of Theorem \ref{thm:ep->acc+m in the next subsection.}

elements.sent-2411-1095: Let $\mathcal{Z}$ be the set of all equivalence classes, i.e. the set of all cycles.
elements.sent-2411-1096: A stable eventually periodic map has the property that the size of each cycle is exactly one.
elements.sent-2411-1097: Additionally, it has no discontinuities in the boundary of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-1098: With this in mind, we define a number which tells us how far an eventually periodic map is from having this property:.
elements.sent-2411-1099: Then the unstable number of $T$, denote by $U(T)$m is defined as: \begin{equation}U(T) \coloneqq \left( \sum_{C \in Z} (|C|-1) \right) + |\partial X \cap C|\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1100: For a periodic discontinuity $\beta$, we will denote by $P(\beta)$ the maximal periodic interval containing $\beta$, in the sense of Definition \ref{defn:max-per-int .}

elements.sent-2411-1102: We say that $T$ has the correspondence property if for each discontinuity $\beta^+ \in X$, we have that $\beta^-$ eventually lands into the maximal periodic interval $P(\beta^+)$ and if analogously for every discontinuity $\betas^- \in X$, $\betas^+$ eventually lands into $P(\betas^-)$.

elements.sent-2411-1406: In this subsection, we elaborate on an example of this construction.
elements.sent-2411-1408: A billiard path in $P$ is the trajectory of a particle moving inside $P$ by straight lines and bouncing off the sides of the polygon according to the physical law of reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.
elements.sent-2411-1409: Such trajectories terminate when they meet the angles of the polygon (see \cite{MR2168892 for a standard introduction to billiards).}
elements.sent-2411-1410: The polygon $P$ is called rational if all its angles are rational multiples of $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-1411: For such polygons, one can associate a canonical surface $S$ with flat Euclidean metric in such a way that every billiard trajectory in $P$ corresponds to a straight line (geodesic) in $S$.
elements.sent-2411-1412: The flat metric on $S$ is defined everywhere except at finitely many cone points, called singularities, for which the cone angles are multiples of $ 2\pi $.
elements.sent-2411-1413: The surface $S$ is constructed by unfolding of $P$ (see \cite{MR0399423, MASUR20021015, MR2809109 for more details of the construction below).}
elements.sent-2411-1414: For each side $e_i$ of $P$, let $ \ell_i \subset \mathbb{R ^2 $ be the line passing through the origin parallel to $e_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-1415: Consider the group $\mathcal{A _P$ generated by reflections $R_{e_i}$ in the line $\ell_i$, where $e_i$ runs over the complete list of edges of $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-1416: Since the angles of $P$ are rational, that is, have the form $2\pi m_i / n_i$ for some coprime integers $m_i < n_i$, the group $\mathcal{A _P$ will contain precisely $2N$ elements, where $N$ is the least common multiple of the $n_i$'s.}
elements.sent-2411-1417: The direction of a billiard trajectory in $P$ is constant between bounces and changes after each bounce by the action of an element in $ \mathcal{A _P $.}
elements.sent-2411-1418: Thus, every billiard trajectory in a rational billiard can have only finitely many directions.
elements.sent-2411-1419: To construct $S$, consider $2N$ polygons of the form $g(P) + v_g$, where $g$ runs over the elements in $\mathcal{A _P$ and the translations $v_g$ are chosen so that the resulting polygons are disjoint; denote them $P_1, \ldots, P_{2N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1420: We now glue the edges of these polygons pairwise to form a surface: for every edge $e_i$ in $P$ and an element $g \in \mathcal{A _P$, consider the element $g \circ R_{e_i \in \mathcal{A _P$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1421: For these two elements, define the edges \begin{equation}\label{Eq:Edges} e' := g(e_i) + v_g \quad \text{ and } \quad e'' := g \circ R_{e_i}(e_i) + v_{g \circ R_{e_i}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-102: \begin{equation}\label{Eq:Edges} e' := g(e_i) + v_g \quad  and  \quad e'' := g \circ R_{e_i}(e_i) + v_{g \circ R_{e_i}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1422: It is easy to check that these edges are parallel and belong to a pair of distinct polygons in the collection $P_1, \ldots, P_{2N}$.
elements.sent-2411-1423: In this way, all edges of the polygons $P_1, \ldots, P_{2N}$ split in such pairs $e', e''$.
elements.sent-2411-1424: We identify these edges by translation (explicitly given by $v_{g \circ R_{e_i - v_g$).}}
elements.sent-2411-1425: After all the identifications, we obtain a closed orientable surface with a flat metric except at finitely many conical singularities (corresponding to the identified vertices of the polygons) where the angles are multiples of $2\pi$ (\cite[Lemma 17.3]{MR2809109 ).}
elements.sent-2411-1426: This is the desired surface $S$; such surfaces are called translation surfaces to indicate that they were obtained by identifying parallel pairs of sides in a collection of polygons by Euclidean translations.
elements.sent-2411-1427: For a given direction $\theta$, the billiard trajectories in $P$ in direction $\theta$ correspond to a parallel geodesic flow $T_\theta $ on $ S $ in direction $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-1428: Each trajectory, instead of reflecting off a side $e$ of $P$, continues as a straight line into the reflected copy of $P$ attached to $P$ through $e$.
elements.sent-2411-1429: This flow is called the billiard flow.
elements.sent-2411-1430: If $I$ is a segment transverse to this flow, then the first return map of $ T_\theta $ to $I$, properly rescaled, is an interval exchange transformation \cite[Section 1.7]{MASUR20021015 .}

elements.sent-2411-1432: Interval translation mappings appear in a similar way once we add spy mirrors to our billiard table.
elements.sent-2411-1433: A spy mirror in $P$ is a two-sided vertical segment, with one transparent side and one reflective side.
elements.sent-2411-1434: The trajectory $\gamma$ of a particle reflects according to the standard rules if it approaches the polygon sides or a spy mirror from its reflective side, and $\gamma $ passes through unobstructed if it approaches a spy mirror from the mirror's transparent side.
elements.sent-2411-1435: A rational billiard with spy mirrors is a rational polygon $P$ together with finitely many spy mirror segments inside $P$ and the reflection law just described; the spy mirrors are assumed to make rational angles with the sides of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-1436: We denote such a billiard table by $\widehat{P}$ \footnote{The polygon $P$ "wears" a hat $ \,\,\widehat{\phantom{ }\,\,\, $, as all typical spies do.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-1437: Unlike classical polygonal billiards, billiards in polygons with spy mirrors are non-invertible dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-1438: We can associate to $\widehat{P}$ a surface $\widehat{S}$ called a translation surface with spy mirrors.

elements.sent-2411-1488: An Interval Translation Mapping (ITM) is a piece-wise translation $T \colon I \to I$ defined on a finite partition $I_1, \ldots, I_r$ of an interval $I$ into $r \ge 2$ sub-intervals.
elements.sent-2411-1489: These maps naturally generalize classical Interval Exchange Transformations (IETs) by removing the bijectivity assumption.
elements.sent-2411-1490: Let $\ITM(r)$ be the space of all interval translation mappings, where we fix $r$ but not the intervals $I_1,\ldots,I_r$, nor the translations on each of them.
elements.sent-2411-1491: The set $X(T) :=\bigcap_{n\ge 0 T^n(I)$ is either a finite union of intervals (and $T$ behaves like an IET on those intervals), in which case the map is called of finite type, or is a disjoint union of finitely many intervals and a Cantor set, in which case the map is called of infinite type.}
elements.sent-2411-1492: In this paper, for an arbitrary $r \ge 2$, we show that the set of finite type maps contains an open and dense set of $\ITM(r)$.
elements.sent-2411-1493: This resolves in positive a topological version of a long-standing conjecture due to Boshernitzan and Kornfeld.
elements.sent-2411-1494: More precisely, we show that there exists an open and dense subset $\mathcal{S (r)$ of $\ITM(r)$ consisting of stable maps such that each $T\in \mathcal{S (r)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1495: Interval Translation Mappings ($\ITM$s) are orientation-preserving piecewise isometries of an interval.
elements.sent-2411-1496: As such, they are the generalization of the well-known Interval Exchange Transformations ($\IET$s) obtained by dropping the bijectivity assumption on the mapping.
elements.sent-2411-1497: They were introduced by Boshernitzan and Kornfeld in \cite{MR1356616 .}

elements.sent-2411-1499: More precisely, an $\ITM$ on $r$ intervals is a map $T \colon [0,1) \to [0,1)$ defined by two sets of parameters:.

elements.sent-2411-1503: Naturally, $\ITM(r)$ is a closed convex polytope in $\mathbb{R ^{2r-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1504: The basic difference between $\ITM$s and $\IET$s is the fact that for a typical $\ITM$ $T$ we have that $T(I) \subsetneq I$, where $I = [0,1)$, which implies that we have the following chain of inclusions: $I \supsetneq T(I) \supseteq T^2(I) \supseteq T^3(I) \supseteq \dots$.
elements.sent-2411-1505: This leads to a natural dichotomy for $\ITM$s: A map $T$ is said to be of finite type if there exists an integer $n$ such that $T^n(I) = T^{n+1 (I)$, and is of infinite type if $T^n(I) \supsetneq T^{n+1 (I)$ for all $n \ge 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1506: The first example of an infinite type map was constructed in \cite{MR1356616 by Boshernitzan and Kornfeld.}
elements.sent-2411-1507: They conjectured that such behavior should be rare: \begin{mconjecture For all $r \ge 2$, the set of all infinite type $\ITM$s on $r$ intervals is a measure zero subset of $\ITM(r)$.}
elements.sent-2411-1508: Namely, it was first established for $r=3$ and a special $2$-parameter family of $\ITM$s in \cite{MR2013352 .}
elements.sent-2411-1509: It was shown that this family supports a renormalization scheme analogous to the Rauzy--Veech induction for $\IET$s (see also \cite{artigiani2024renormalization ).}
elements.sent-2411-1510: The dynamical properties of this renormalization imply that the measure of the set of all infinite type maps must be zero, analogous to how the analysis of the Rauzy--Veech induction shows that almost all minimal $\IET$s are uniquely ergodic (see \cite{MR644018 , \cite{MR644019 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-1511: A similar method of renormalization was used for $r \le 4$ and a more complicated family of double rotations in \cite{MR2152403 and \cite{MR2966738 (see also \cite{MR4397159 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1512: The conjecture was established in full for $r=3$ in \cite{MR3124735 by showing that almost every $\ITM$ on three intervals can be renormalized to a double rotation.}
elements.sent-2411-1513: Unfortunately, there has been little progress on developing a renormalization scheme for $\ITM$s on an arbitrary number of intervals (except in some special cases \cite{MR2308208 ).}
elements.sent-2411-1514: In this paper, instead of using renormalization, we develop a theory of stability for $\ITM$s (Section \ref{sec:theoremB ) and show a transversality result that allows for detailed control over global dynamics under perturbation (Section \ref{sec:lin-indep , Theorem \ref{thm:lin-dep ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1515: Using these results, we establish the topological version of Boshernitzan--Kornfeld Conjecture, the main result of this paper: \begin{mtheorem For every $r \ge 2$, the set of all finite type $\ITM$s on $r$ intervals contains an open and dense subset of $\ITM(r)$.}

elements.subsection-2411-027: Future work: geometry and dynamics in the boundary of stable regions
elements.sent-2411-1696: In this final subsection, we discuss the properties of the full parameter space $\ITM(r)$ of all $\ITM$s on $r$ intervals, and we present a few conjectures.
elements.sent-2411-1697: Recall that a connected component of the set of stable maps $\mathcal{S (r)$ corresponds to the maximal neighbourhood $[T]$ of a stable map $T$ that satisfies the three properties from the definition of stability (Definition \ref{defn:stable ).}}
elements.sent-2411-1698: A very interesting problem is to describe the geometry of these regions.
elements.sent-2411-1699: Since ghost preimages are codimension $1$ phenomena, it seems plausible that by taking the closure of a stable region, we can make it convex.
elements.sent-2411-1700: The accumulation properties of these hyperplanes seem difficult to describe, and we are led to the following question: \begin{question Is the boundary of every stable region locally connected? \end{question Besides their geometry, a step towards the proof of Boshernitzan--Kornfeld Conjecture is to understand how the stable regions are arranged in the parameter $\ITM(r)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1701: Are they necessarily separated from each other, or can they `tile' the parameter space? \begin{question Are the stable regions (partially) separated from each other? In other words, does for every stable region $[T]$ exist an open subset of the (outer) boundary on which the set of points that are contained in the boundary of some other stable component is small (e.g. meager or countable)? \end{question Figure \ref{fig:bt-special-family suggests that this is true: every closed coloured triangle has at least one side on which the set of points where it touches other triangles is countable.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1702: Another interesting problem is describing the dynamics in the boundary of stable regions: \begin{question What are the dynamical properties of a map in the boundary of a stable region? \end{question The boundary of a stable set is where the topology of a non-wandering set changes, so it is contained in bifurcation locus of $\ITM(r)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1703: Thus the answer to this question would constitute the start of the bifurcation theory of $\ITM$s.
elements.sent-2411-1704: Intuitively, the bifurcations should be associated with the changes of critical itineraries, and they should in turn be associated with critical connections.
elements.sent-2411-1705: This is confirmed by Theorem \ref{thm:cc-in-bdry for finite type maps.}
elements.sent-2411-1706: It is not clear that this should also hold for infinite-type maps: \begin{question Do infinite type maps in the boundary of a stable region have critical connections? \end{question Since a necessary condition for a finite type map to be in the boundary of a stable region is that it has a critical connection, a natural question to ask is whether the maximal possible number of critical connections is sufficient for a map to be in the closure of a stable region.}}
elements.sent-2411-1707: Thus it is possible that by making the perturbation smaller, we end up in different stable regions.. .
